“Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) in Python — A Beginner’s Guide”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

By Manikanta Pavuluri

Introduction
When I started learning Python, I was comfortable writing simple programs — printing messages, using loops, and creating functions. But as soon as someone mentioned Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), I felt completely lost.
Words like class, object, inheritance, and polymorphism sounded like a foreign language to me.
I remember thinking, “Is this really necessary? Can’t I just keep writing code the normal way?”
But as I worked on bigger projects, I started to see the problem. My code was becoming messy, repetitive, and very hard to manage. That’s when OOP finally started to make sense.
In this blog, I’m sharing what I learned about OOP in Python in the simplest way possible — so that every beginner can understand it without any confusion.

In this article, we’ll explore:
✔️ What OOP is and why it matters in Python.
✔️ What classes and objects are.
✔️ The four main pillars of OOP — with simple examples.
✔️ Real-world applications of OOP.
✔️ Common mistakes beginners make.
By the end of this article, you’ll have a clear understanding of OOP and feel confident enough to start using it in your own Python programs.

What is Object-Oriented Programming?
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a way of writing code that organizes everything around objects.
An object is simply a collection of data (called attributes) and actions (called methods) that belong together.
In simple words, OOP helps us model real-world things in our code.

A Real-Life Example
Think about a car.
Every car has:

• Attributes — color, brand, speed, fuel level.

• Actions — start, stop, accelerate, brake.
In OOP, we create a class that acts like a blueprint for a car. Then, we create objects from that blueprint — just like a factory produces multiple cars from the same design.
This is the core idea behind OOP.

Why OOP is Important in Python
After struggling with large programs, I realized that OOP solves many real problems that beginners face.
1. It Organizes Code Better
OOP groups related data and functions together inside a class. This makes the code much cleaner and easier to navigate.
2. It Reduces Repetition
We write the blueprint once (as a class) and create as many objects as we need.
3. It Makes Code Easier to Maintain
If we need to change something, we update it in one class instead of searching through hundreds of lines.
4. It Models Real-World Problems
OOP is perfect for building systems that mirror real life — like banking apps, hospital management systems, and e-commerce platforms.
5. It Makes Large Projects Manageable
Almost every professional Python project — from web development to data science — uses OOP.

Classes and Objects — The Foundation of OOP
What is a Class?
A class is a blueprint or template that defines attributes and methods.
class Car:
def __init__(self, brand, color):
self.brand = brand
self.color = color def start(self):
print(f"The {self.color} {self.brand} is starting!")What is an Object?
An object is a real instance created from a class.
my_car = Car("Toyota", "Red")
my_car.start()Output
The Red Toyota is starting!Here:

• Car is the class (the blueprint).

• my_car is the object (the actual car created from the blueprint).

• __init__ is a special method called the constructor — it runs automatically when an object is created.

• self refers to the current object itself.
When I first saw self, it confused me a lot. But think of it as the object saying "this is my brand, my color."

The Four Pillars of OOP
OOP is built on four main concepts. Let me explain each one with a simple example.

1. Encapsulation — Keeping Data Safe
Encapsulation means hiding the internal details of an object and only exposing what is necessary.
Think of an ATM machine. You can withdraw money by pressing a button — but you don’t see all the complex code running behind the scenes. That’s encapsulation.
class BankAccount:
def __init__(self, owner, balance):
self.owner = owner
self.__balance = balance # private attribute def deposit(self, amount):
self.__balance += amount
print(f"₹{amount} deposited. New balance: ₹{self.__balance}") def get_balance(self):
return self.__balanceaccount = BankAccount("Manikanta", 5000)
account.deposit(2000)
print("Balance:", account.get_balance())Output
₹2000 deposited. New balance: ₹7000
Balance: 7000The __balance with double underscore is a private attribute — it cannot be accessed directly from outside the class. This protects the data.

2. Inheritance — Reusing Code from a Parent Class
Inheritance allows one class to take on the properties and methods of another class.
Think of it like a family. A child inherits traits from their parents — but they can also have their own unique traits.
class Animal:
def __init__(self, name):
self.name = name def speak(self):
print(f"{self.name} makes a sound.")class Dog(Animal):
def speak(self):
print(f"{self.name} says: Woof!")class Cat(Animal):
def speak(self):
print(f"{self.name} says: Meow!")dog = Dog("Bruno")
cat = Cat("Whiskers")dog.speak()
cat.speak()Output
Bruno says: Woof!
Whiskers says: Meow!Here:

• Animal is the parent class (also called base class).

• Dog and Cat are child classes (also called derived classes).

• Both inherit the __init__ method from Animal, saving us from writing it again.
Inheritance was the concept that made me realize how much time OOP can save in large projects.

3. Polymorphism — One Action, Many Forms
Polymorphism means the same method can behave differently in different classes.
The word comes from Greek — poly means many, morphism means forms.
class Shape:
def area(self):
passclass Circle(Shape):
def __init__(self, radius):
self.radius = radius def area(self):
return 3.14 * self.radius * self.radiusclass Rectangle(Shape):
def __init__(self, length, width):
self.length = length
self.width = width def area(self):
return self.length * self.widthshapes = [Circle(7), Rectangle(5, 10)]for shape in shapes:
print("Area:", shape.area())Output
Area: 153.86
Area: 50The same method area() behaves differently for Circle and Rectangle. That is polymorphism.

4. Abstraction — Hiding Complexity
Abstraction means showing only the important features and hiding the unnecessary details.
Think of a smartphone. You just tap an app icon — you don’t need to understand the millions of lines of code running underneath.
from abc import ABC, abstractmethodclass Vehicle(ABC):
@abstractmethod
def fuel_type(self):
passclass ElectricCar(Vehicle):
def fuel_type(self):
print("This car runs on electricity.")class PetrolCar(Vehicle):
def fuel_type(self):
print("This car runs on petrol.")car1 = ElectricCar()
car2 = PetrolCar()car1.fuel_type()
car2.fuel_type()Output
This car runs on electricity.
This car runs on petrol.ABC stands for Abstract Base Class. An abstract method is like a rule — every child class must define it in their own way.

Real-World Applications of OOP
When I was learning OOP, I thought it was only useful for practice problems. But later I realized that OOP is everywhere in the real world.
Banking Applications

• Each bank account is an object with attributes like owner name, balance, and account number.

• Methods like deposit, withdraw, and check balance are defined inside the class.
Hospital Management Systems

• Patients, doctors, and appointments are all objects.

• OOP helps manage complex relationships between them.
E-commerce Websites

• Products, customers, carts, and orders are all modeled as objects.
Game Development

• Every character, enemy, weapon, and level is an object with its own attributes and actions.
Data Science and Machine Learning

• Machine learning models in libraries like Scikit-learn are built using OOP.

• When you call model.fit() or model.predict(), you are using OOP methods.

Advantages of OOP
✅ Code is cleaner and more organized
✅ Easy to reuse code through inheritance
✅ Easier to debug and maintain
✅ Models real-world problems naturally
✅ Makes large projects manageable
✅ Used in almost every professional Python project

Common Mistakes Beginners Make
Forgetting self as the First Parameter
Wrong:
def greet():
print("Hello")Correct:
def greet(self):
print("Hello")Forgetting the Colon After Class Definition
Wrong:
class StudentCorrect:
class Student:Forgetting to Call super().__init__() in Child Class
Wrong:
class Dog(Animal):
def __init__(self, name, breed):
self.breed = breed # name won't be initialized!Correct:
class Dog(Animal):
def __init__(self, name, breed):
super().__init__(name) # initializes the parent class first
self.breed = breedI made all of these mistakes myself when I was learning. Fixing them one by one helped me understand OOP much better.

Final Example — Putting It All Together
Let’s create a simple Student Management System using OOP.
class Student:
def __init__(self, name, roll_number, marks):
self.name = name
self.roll_number = roll_number
self.__marks = marks # encapsulated def get_marks(self):
return self.__marks def display(self):
print(f"Name: {self.name} | Roll No: {self.roll_number} | Marks: {self.__marks}") def grade(self):
if self.__marks >= 90:
return "A"
elif self.__marks >= 75:
return "B"
elif self.__marks >= 60:
return "C"
else:
return "F"s1 = Student("Manikanta", 101, 85)
s2 = Student("Ravi", 102, 58)s1.display()
print("Grade:", s1.grade())s2.display()
print("Grade:", s2.grade())Output
Name: Manikanta | Roll No: 101 | Marks: 85
Grade: B
Name: Ravi | Roll No: 102 | Marks: 58
Grade: FThis small example uses encapsulation (private marks), methods for behavior, and logic inside the class — all the building blocks of OOP in one place.

Conclusion
Learning OOP was one of those moments in my Python journey where everything started clicking.
At first, I thought functions were enough. But as I built bigger programs, I realized that OOP is what separates beginner code from professional code.
Understanding the four pillars — Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction — gave me the confidence to look at any Python project and understand how it’s structured.
I’m still learning and growing, but OOP has definitely made me a more organized and confident programmer. If you’re a beginner, my advice is simple: start small, build one class at a time, and practice every day.
The more you use OOP, the more natural it will feel.
Every big Python project — web apps, data science tools, machine learning models — is built using these same concepts.
Learn the blueprint, build the objects, and let your code do the work — that’s the real power of OOP in Python.
Happy Coding….! 🐍
